AI on the
Org Chart

What I learned building a post-agentic organization

GCP AI Summit · June 9, 2026 · Mel McGee, CEO, SkillSpout

Last year, I hit a wall.

Not a dramatic one — the slow kind, where you spend more time managing context than doing the actual work.

I run two companies — three email accounts, sales and finance and product and a team, all going at once. I was already using AI every day, the usual tools.

There's a difference between using AI and building it into how you operate. So I rebuilt my whole operation around it.

Orientation
Five stages.
Where are you?

A quick map before we start. I built it to teach clients, then realized I’d have to walk it myself. Find yourself on it — then watch me climb it.

The five stages of AI maturity

Each stage fails in a predictable place. Knowing where saves you a quarter of wasted momentum.

Stage 1
Foundational AI
Literacy, safety, and shared vocabulary.
Where it breaksTraining ends and nothing changes, because nobody connected skills to real work.
Leaders underestimateFear. People hear "your task was automated" as "you are replaceable."
Stage 2
Applied AI
Practical workflows and working policy.
Where it breaksUsage stays individual. Ten private productivity hacks never become one shared workflow.
Leaders underestimateHow fast policy questions arrive once real work flows through AI.
Stage 3
Embedded AI
Department workflows and role assistants.
Where it breaksAssistants get built and abandoned, because nobody owns keeping context current.
Leaders underestimateContext. The assistant is only as good as what the department wrote down.
Stage 4
Agentic AI
Multi-step workflows that act across systems.
Where it breaksAgents act faster than anyone can review. Evaluation collapses before value arrives.
Leaders underestimateGovernance. Autonomy must be earned per workflow, never batch-granted.
Stage 5
Cultural AI
The organization redesigns around the new layer.
Where it breaksReverting. Without named owners and learning loops, the gains slip back and people drift to old habits.
Leaders underestimateThat this stage is an org-design project, not an IT project.
Show of hands
Where is your organization today, honestly? Hands up as I call each stage.
01 · What I Built
Evidence,
not theory.

I rebuilt my own operations around this architecture. The hard part was never the technology; the hard part is changing how you work. Here is the anatomy, and one workflow running end to end.

This is the actual thing.

Not a mockup and not the terminal in the video — this is the interface I work in every day, reviewing what the system drafted.

“

Post-agentic does not mean agents are over. It means agents are no longer the novelty. They are assumed infrastructure, and the organization has been redesigned around that reality.

The question stops being "where do we add an agent?"
It becomes "how is work assembled, executed, governed, and improved, and who remains accountable?"

"AI-native" does not mean what the vendors say it means.

01
It is not
Everyone has a Copilot license
Seat counts measure procurement, not transformation.
02
It is not
A chatbot in every department
Scattered assistants automate fragments while the workflow stays broken.
03
It is not
Maximum agent count
Deploying agents you cannot evaluate is risk, not progress.
04
It is not
Accountability outsourced to the machine
A person must own the objective, the boundaries, and the quality bar.

An AI-native organization is one in which intent is the entry point. Five properties define it.

  • Intent is the entry point. You express the outcome; the system assembles the work.
  • Context is persistent. The organization's knowledge is structured and reachable.
  • Execution is composable. Skills chain into workflows instead of one-off prompts.
  • Autonomy is governed. Trust is earned per workflow, not granted by enthusiasm.
  • Every completed workflow can make the system smarter.
19%
of AI users sit in Microsoft's "Frontier zone": high individual capability and high organizational readiness.
26%
say leadership is clearly and consistently aligned on AI.

Source: Microsoft Work Trend Index, May 5, 2026.

Five layers, from model to accountability

Think of it like a capable colleague: a brain, what it knows, a body to act, trained skills, and the rules it works under. AI is not so different — only the brain is rented. You supply the intent, and the one thing it can never have is accountability.

1
Modelthe thinking
The brain’s raw thinking. Rented, improving, and the same one your competitor can rent — brilliant, but it shows up knowing nothing about you.
2
Contextthe AI Brain
What it knows and remembers: your data, your knowledge, your voice — everything you’ve learned about your business. The part that’s yours, and the one layer that compounds.
3
Harnessthe body
Hands, senses, and nerves: the tools, retrieval, and connections (MCP) that let the brain reach your knowledge and act. A brilliant mind with no body does nothing.
4
Operating systemskills & habits
Trained, repeatable methods: reusable skills and workflow chains. The craft you pick up in school and on the job — how the work gets done the same way every time.
5
Governancethe rules
Laws and accountability: what it is allowed to do, where a human approves, and who answers for it. The one human thing it can’t have — so a person keeps it.

You have been the integration layer.

BeforeApplication-centric work
Email
Calendar
CRM
Shared drives
Project system
Dashboards
YOU, manually reconstructing context
The lines on your org chart have always been powered by humans. People are the middleware connecting badly integrated systems, and every consequential decision starts with a tour of six applications.
AfterIntent-centric work
Human intent
"Prepare me for the 2 PM meeting."
AI operating layer
Assembles context across connected systems
Systems and agents
CRM, email, documents, research, workflows
Proposed or completed work
Briefings, drafts, updates, next actions
Human judgment
Decisions, exceptions, approvals

We are moving from application-centric work to intent-centric work. The applications remain systems of record. The AI layer becomes the system of interaction.

A sales meeting, the post-agentic way

1 · Sense
Meeting detected
A sales meeting appears on the calendar. No one asks the system to start.
2 · Assemble
Context gathers 7 sources
CRM, email, calendar, prior transcripts, LinkedIn, the web, our own docs — in our voice.
3 · Deliver
Briefing arrives
A meeting brief lands before the call. The human walks in prepared.
4 · Process
Transcript worked
After the meeting, the system drafts CRM updates, tasks, and follow-up email.
5 · Trust gate
Everything pauses
Nothing external sends without human approval. Every time. By design.
6 · Learn
Outcome captured
What happened feeds back into the workflow, so the next run is smarter.

Six moves, one workflow. Watch what it demonstrates as we step through: the inversion, the glue, the composition, and the human exactly where judgment matters.

The terminal is not the story.
The movement of work is the story.

Recorded run · Sanitized data
Four minutes, real workflow, no edits.
Watch forContext arriving assembled. Nobody opens six applications.
Watch forThe pause at the trust boundary before anything external moves.
Watch forThe outcome prompt at the end. The system asks what happened.

When the system does the doing,
you become the bottleneck.

6 AM
briefing runs itself
The morning briefing is ready before anyone wakes up.
Zero
manual CRM entries
Follow-through is drafted by the system, approved by a human.
10-15x
my own time, on a good day
The constraint on doing the work is essentially gone.

So I became the constraint. The work that's left is judgment, and reviewing the AI's work is a skill in its own right. The AI takes the first pass; a human takes the last pass. Every time.

Internal figures from system run records. Client example: a 78% proposal-time reduction at Milia Marketing after workflow redesign.

02 · Where It Goes
Where AI
actually goes.

Two structural changes explain almost everything that feels different about working this way.

Where does AI go on the org chart?

Most leaders answer literally: a job title, a seat, a box. A randomized study of 1,261 managers shows what happens when they do — in the organizations that have already put AI on the chart, naming agents as employees made managers catch fewer errors, pass more of the review to others, and quietly shift accountability off themselves and onto the machine.

16%
weaker oversight
Managers reviewed an “AI employee” 16% less closely than the same work from a tool — and caught 18% fewer errors.
+44%
passing the buck
They leaned far harder on someone else to check it: a 22-point jump in requests for additional review.
−9 pts
accountability lost
They held themselves less accountable and pushed responsibility onto the AI system.

The instinct is right. The geometry is wrong. An agent doesn't get a box on your org chart — no title, no seat, no role of its own. It gets an owner: a human accountable for its work, the way a manager answers for their team. The boxes stay human, because accountability stays human.

Source: Wiles, Hsu, Bedard & Kropp, “Putting AI on the Org Chart: Evidence on Delegation and Oversight” — randomized study of 1,261 managers (emmawiles.com/storage/ai_employee.pdf; also via MIT IDE and BCG / Harvard Business Review). Figures are for managers in organizations that already list AI agents on their charts; average effects across all managers were small. Framing identical work as an “AI employee” (vs a tool) cut monitoring ~16% and errors caught ~18%, raised reliance on outside review ~44% (22 pts), and moved ~9 points of accountability from the manager to the AI — with no gain in adoption. Related BCG/HBR coverage links the same framing to higher job-replacement fear and lower trust.

Where it goes·The agent reports to a human

The org chart answers
"who reports to whom."

It cannot answer how an outcome gets produced, where AI acts, where humans review, or who is accountable when an agent makes a mistake.

An agent never gets its own box. It is a tool people use, not a teammate to blame — it can’t hold accountability the way a person can. One human owns it; everyone who uses it owns their results.

CEO
Sales
Marketing
Operations
"Kevin,"
AI analyst
The familiar abstraction: authority, departments, reporting lines.
Owned
Sarah · COO
AgentEngagement Health Monitor
One named human is accountable. If it breaks, she works with the builder to fix it.
Used
RepRepRep
AgentShared drafting tool
Many people use it. Each owns their own results. No one blames the tool.

I told it to fix itself. It took ownership.

Building a skill to fill a government form and fax it back. It was placing fields wrong, so I told it to test itself and iterate. “Taking ownership of this… iterate with my own screenshots until it’s correct.”

How to put AI on the org chart, correctly

  • Agents live in a registry, not on reporting lines. Inventory entries with permissions, prompts, and scope. This is where the research points: govern agents as inventory, not identities.
  • Name agents after workflows, never personas. "Engagement Health Monitor," not "Kevin."
  • Every agent gets one named human owner. The single point every 2026 governance framework converges on.
  • Autonomy is tiered and earned. Trust levels per agent, raised only on observed reliability.
  • Set the human-AI split per workstream, not company-wide. In each one, decide what the AI drafts and where a human leads. Most stay human-led, on purpose.
# agent registry — live entry from our system
name: Engagement Health Monitor
type: agent # not a person
owner: sarah # one named human
trust_tier: 1 # earned, not granted
schedule: Mondays 5:30 AM
workflow: engagement lifecycle health
gate: exceptions escalate to the owner

A real entry from our own team roster. The agent gets governance. The boxes stay human.

This is teachable
Agent governance and human-in-the-loop design are the curriculum inside Stages 4 and 5 of our maturity framework. Organizations do not have to invent this from scratch.

The rules, running on their own.

It ran the PII gate, classified the document, pushed it to the right shared drive — then noticed I use that route often and offered to add it to the index. Governance and self-improvement, unprompted.

03 · What I Learned
Five lessons,
earned the hard way.

What we would tell you before you build

Lesson 1
Workflow comes before agent.
Do not ask "where can we add an agent?" Ask what outcome you are producing, where it stalls, and what done means. Automating a poorly understood process gives you a faster, less visible mess.
Lesson 2
Context is the asset. Software is becoming ephemeral.
Models commoditize; your context compounds. The quality of every answer depends on what your organization wrote down. If it is not recorded, it did not happen, as far as your AI is concerned.
Lesson 3
Autonomy is a dial, not a switch.
Low-risk, reversible work runs automatically. External, irreversible actions get approved one at a time. New capabilities start gated and earn autonomy through observed reliability, not executive enthusiasm.
Lesson 4
The loop only learns what you tell it happened.
Microsoft reports active agents grew 15x year over year. Execution is easy to log. Outcomes are not: our own outcome capture ran at 3% while execution capture was near total. The constraint on a self-improving organization is not intelligence. It is outcome capture.
Lesson 5
You can't manage an agent you can't see into.
Every agent has one accountable human — but accountability without visibility is fake. If the people who build the agent work in a vacuum, the owner can't tell when it's off, can't give feedback, can't fix what's broken. You don't put an agent on a performance improvement plan; you talk to its builder. The owner and the builder have to stay in contact — and everyone has to know when to escalate. Ownership without a line to the builder is just a name on a box.

Agent growth: Microsoft Work Trend Index, May 5, 2026, first-party platform telemetry (18x in large enterprises). Outcome-capture figure: internal system audit, March 2026.

The loop, live.

It flagged a pattern across eight sessions and asked to govern it — then asked how the session felt. That feedback is how the system learns how I work, and how I grow. Grist for the mill.

Start with exactly one workflow.

01
One workflow · high-frequency, cross-system, painful, measurable
02
One accountable owner · a named person, never a committee
03
One explicit trust boundary · where the human approves, judges, or escalates
04
One measurable outcome · decided before the build, not after
05
Twenty observed runs · before you scale anything

Do not scale the prototype. Scale the validated operating pattern.

These are principles, not a product. You don’t need my stack to run them — build it however fits your organization.

The winners will not be the companies with the most agents. They will be the organizations that turn judgment into reusable context, workflows into learning systems, and autonomy into something earned.

So when someone asks where AI sits on your org chart, you now have the real answer: it doesn't get a box. It gets an owner — a human accountable for its work. The boxes stay human, because accountability stays human.

That is the shift from adopting AI to building a post-agentic organization.

Thank you.
Mel McGee · CEO, SkillSpout
